articles.headline: Human Rights Day 2024: India Reiterates Commitment Amidst Persistent Challenges
articles.explainer:
• December 10 marks Human Rights Day, commemorating the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) in 1948.
• India, a signatory to the UDHR, reaffirms its constitutional commitment to fundamental rights, enshrined in Part III of its Constitution.
• Discussions focus on progress in areas like right to education, food security, and judicial reforms, alongside challenges in protecting vulnerable groups, ensuring free speech, and addressing custodial violence.
• The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) emphasizes its role in monitoring and promoting human rights across the nation.
• International calls for greater accountability and adherence to global human rights standards are also highlighted.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: Human Rights Day is observed annually on December 10.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948.
India is a founding member of the UN and a signatory to the UDHR.
Part III of the Indian Constitution enshrines Fundamental Rights.
The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a statutory body established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze India's progress and challenges in upholding human rights since its independence, particularly in the context of the UDHR principles.
Discuss the role of the NHRC and other institutions in safeguarding human rights in India, identifying their strengths and limitations.
Examine the interplay between national sovereignty and international human rights obligations, using India as a case study.
Propose policy recommendations to enhance the protection of human rights for marginalized and vulnerable communities in India.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of diverse hands holding a globe, symbolizing universal human rights.
articles.trivia: The UDHR is the most translated document in the world, available in over 500 languages.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Fundamental Rights and Human Rights Bodies

articles.headline: Nobel Peace Prize 2024 Awarded in Oslo Ceremony
articles.explainer:
• The Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 was formally presented in Oslo, Norway, on December 10, following the annual tradition.
• The recipient(s) were recognized for their outstanding contributions to peace, disarmament, or human rights.
• The ceremony highlighted the global challenges demanding peaceful resolutions and humanitarian efforts.
• The Nobel Committee's selection often aims to bring attention to pressing global issues and inspire further action.
• This year's award underscored the critical importance of international cooperation in an increasingly complex geopolitical landscape.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: The Nobel Peace Prize is awarded annually in Oslo, Norway.
The other Nobel Prizes (Physics, Chemistry, Physiology or Medicine, Literature, and Economic Sciences) are awarded in Stockholm, Sweden.
Alfred Nobel, the founder of the awards, specified the Peace Prize to be awarded by a Norwegian committee.
Mother Teresa (1979) and Kailash Satyarthi (2014) are Indian Nobel Peace laureates.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical significance and impact of the Nobel Peace Prize in shaping global peace efforts and human rights advocacy.
Discuss the criteria and controversies often associated with the selection of Nobel Peace laureates, and their implications for international politics.
Evaluate the role of non-state actors and civil society organizations in promoting peace, as often recognized by the Nobel Peace Prize.
articles.imageDescription: Image of the Nobel Peace Prize medal and diploma.
articles.trivia: The Nobel Peace Prize has been withheld on 19 occasions, mostly during World Wars.

articles.headline: Winter Session: Parliament Debates Key Bills on Digital Data Protection and Cooperative Sector Reforms
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Parliament's Winter Session continues, with significant legislative activity focused on the Digital Personal Data Protection Bill, 2023, and amendments to the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act.
• The data protection bill aims to establish a comprehensive legal framework for the processing of digital personal data, ensuring privacy rights of individuals.
• Cooperative sector reforms seek to enhance governance, management, and financial health of cooperative societies across states.
• Opposition parties raise concerns over consultation processes and potential impacts on fundamental rights and federal structure.
• The government emphasizes the necessity of these reforms for economic growth and citizen welfare in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Bill aims to regulate digital personal data processing.
It includes provisions for the establishment of a Data Protection Board of India.
Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act governs cooperatives operating in more than one state.
Winter Session of Parliament typically runs from November to December.
A Bill passed by both Houses of Parliament requires Presidential assent to become an Act.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the potential impact of the Digital Personal Data Protection Bill on individual privacy, economic innovation, and government surveillance in India.
Analyze the need for reforms in the cooperative sector and discuss how the proposed amendments to the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act address existing challenges.
Discuss the role of parliamentary debates in shaping significant legislation and ensuring democratic accountability, citing examples from the ongoing Winter Session.
Examine the balance between state regulation and individual autonomy in the context of digital governance and data privacy.
articles.imageDescription: Image of the Parliament House of India, illuminated.
articles.trivia: The first Lok Sabha had its inaugural session on 13 May 1952.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Global Uncertainties and Domestic Inflation Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its latest review, deciding to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged.
• The decision comes against a backdrop of persistent global economic uncertainties, including geopolitical tensions and fluctuating commodity prices.
• Domestic inflation, particularly in food items, remains a key concern, though core inflation shows signs of moderation.
• The RBI emphasized its commitment to achieving the medium-term inflation target while supporting economic growth.
• Experts anticipate a 'wait and watch' approach from the central bank, with future rate cuts dependent on sustained disinflation and global economic stability.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body headed by the RBI Governor.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims for CPI inflation of 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Quantitative easing and tightening are tools used by central banks.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate, considering both domestic and international economic factors.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with the objective of supporting economic growth in the current macroeconomic environment.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflationary pressures, particularly in food items.
Examine the impact of global economic uncertainties, such as geopolitical conflicts and commodity price volatility, on India's monetary policy decisions.
articles.imageDescription: Image of the Reserve Bank of India headquarters building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The RBI was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: India-GCC Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Advance, Aiming for Early Conclusion
articles.explainer:
• Negotiations for a Free Trade Agreement (FTA) between India and the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) are progressing positively, with both sides aiming for an early conclusion.
• The proposed FTA is expected to significantly boost bilateral trade and investment, especially in energy, infrastructure, and services sectors.
• The GCC, comprising six West Asian countries, is a crucial partner for India's energy security and a major destination for Indian expatriates.
• Discussions focus on market access for goods and services, investment protection, and dispute resolution mechanisms.
• An FTA is anticipated to diversify trade baskets and strengthen strategic ties between India and the GCC member states.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) includes Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and UAE.
India is a major trading partner for the GCC countries.
FTAs aim to reduce tariffs and non-tariff barriers to trade.
India's energy imports significantly rely on GCC nations.
Remittances from the Indian diaspora in the GCC are a major source of foreign exchange for India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of an India-GCC Free Trade Agreement for India's 'Look West' policy and energy security.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges that such an FTA could bring to various sectors of the Indian economy, including manufacturing, services, and agriculture.
Examine how deepening economic ties with the GCC can contribute to India's broader geopolitical objectives in the West Asian region.
Evaluate the role of the Indian diaspora in strengthening economic and cultural linkages between India and the GCC countries.
articles.imageDescription: Map highlighting India and the Gulf Cooperation Council countries.
articles.trivia: The GCC was established in 1981.

articles.headline: National Education Policy 2020: Ministry Reviews Implementation Progress and Challenges
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education held a high-level meeting to review the implementation status of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 across states and Union Territories.
• Key areas of focus included the integration of vocational education, foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN), and the multi-disciplinary approach in higher education.
• Challenges such as infrastructure gaps, faculty training, and digital divide in remote areas were deliberated.
• The review highlighted successful initiatives in some states and stressed the need for collaborative efforts to achieve NEP's transformative goals.
• Emphasis was placed on leveraging technology for effective learning and assessment, aligning with the vision of 'Digital India'.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
It proposes a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education.
Vocational education is to be integrated from Class 6 onwards.
Academic Bank of Credits (ABC) is a key feature for higher education.
The policy aims for 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in preschool to secondary level by 2030.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the progress and challenges in implementing the National Education Policy 2020, particularly concerning foundational literacy and vocational integration.
Discuss how NEP 2020 aims to address the skill gap and enhance employability among Indian youth, and what further measures are needed.
Examine the role of technology and digital initiatives in achieving the objectives of NEP 2020, especially in bridging the urban-rural education divide.
Suggest strategies for effective federal cooperation between the Centre and states to ensure uniform and successful implementation of the NEP.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing the future of education.
articles.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's education policies, including the first National Policy on Education in 1968.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Expresses Concern Over Delay in Judicial Appointments, Reiterates Collegium Recommendations
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India expressed strong reservations over the Union government's continued delay in clearing judicial appointments and transfers recommended by the Collegium.
• The Court reiterated the importance of adhering to the timelines set for the appointment process to ensure the efficient functioning of the judiciary.
• Vacancies in High Courts and the Supreme Court are impacting the pace of justice delivery, leading to increased pendency of cases.
• The issue highlights ongoing friction between the judiciary and the executive regarding the appointment mechanism.
• The Court emphasized that the independence of the judiciary is a cornerstone of the Constitution and must be preserved.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary; Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
prelimsPoints: The Collegium System is the system for appointment and transfer of judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.
It evolved through Supreme Court judgments (First, Second, and Third Judges Cases).
The Chief Justice of India (CJI) heads the Supreme Court Collegium.
Article 124 and Article 217 deal with the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court and High Courts, respectively.
The National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) Act was struck down by the Supreme Court in 2015.
mainsPoints: Critically examine the Collegium system for judicial appointments in India, highlighting its merits and demerits in ensuring judicial independence and accountability.
Discuss the implications of delayed judicial appointments on the efficiency of the justice delivery system and the concept of rule of law in India.
Analyze the ongoing debate between the executive and judiciary regarding judicial appointments, suggesting possible reforms to streamline the process.
Evaluate the principle of separation of powers in the context of judicial appointments and the need for a balanced approach between different organs of the government.
articles.imageDescription: Façade of the Supreme Court of India building.
articles.trivia: The Supreme Court of India officially came into being on January 28, 1950.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Appointment of Judges and Collegium System

articles.headline: India AI Mission Launched to Boost AI Research, Development, and Ethical Deployment
articles.explainer:
• The Indian government officially launched the 'India AI Mission', a comprehensive initiative aimed at accelerating research, development, and ethical deployment of Artificial Intelligence.
• The mission includes establishing Centers of Excellence for AI, fostering AI startups, developing high-end computing infrastructure, and creating a robust data ecosystem.
• Emphasis is placed on responsible AI development, addressing ethical concerns, data privacy, and potential societal impacts.
• The initiative seeks to position India as a global leader in AI innovation, contributing to various sectors like healthcare, agriculture, and governance.
• Public-private partnerships and international collaborations are envisioned as key pillars for the mission's success.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: India AI Mission aims to boost AI capabilities across sectors.
It focuses on R&D, infrastructure, and ethical considerations.
MeitY is the nodal ministry for AI development in India.
AI applications are being explored in healthcare, agriculture, education, and governance.
National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence was released in 2018.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key components of the 'India AI Mission' and its potential to transform various sectors of the Indian economy and society.
Discuss the ethical challenges associated with the widespread adoption of Artificial Intelligence and how India's mission plans to address them.
Evaluate India's current standing in the global AI landscape and suggest strategies to achieve leadership in specific AI domains through the mission.
Examine the potential impact of AI on employment patterns, skill requirements, and the future of work in India, and how the mission can mitigate negative consequences.
articles.imageDescription: Stylized graphic representing Artificial Intelligence with circuits and a brain icon.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

articles.headline: Project Dolphin: Conservation Efforts Intensified for National Aquatic Animal
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change announced intensified efforts under 'Project Dolphin' to protect the Gangetic Dolphin, India's National Aquatic Animal.
• Measures include habitat protection, curbing poaching, reducing by-catch, and raising awareness among local communities along riverine ecosystems.
• The Gangetic Dolphin, an indicator species of river health, faces threats from pollution, dam construction, and unsustainable fishing practices.
• Advanced technologies, including satellite tagging and eDNA monitoring, are being employed for better population assessment and tracking.
• Collaboration with riparian states and international bodies is crucial for the long-term survival of the species.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Bio-diversity and Climate Change.
prelimsPoints: The Gangetic Dolphin (Platanista gangetica) is India's National Aquatic Animal.
It is listed as 'Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
Project Dolphin was launched by the Prime Minister in 2020.
It is a freshwater dolphin found in the Ganga-Brahmaputra-Meghna river system.
Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Sanctuary in Bihar is India's only dolphin sanctuary.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of the Gangetic Dolphin as an indicator species and the various threats it faces in its natural habitat.
Evaluate the effectiveness of 'Project Dolphin' in conserving the species, outlining its key strategies and the challenges in their implementation.
Examine the role of community participation and inter-state cooperation in successful wildlife conservation efforts, with specific reference to riverine species.
Suggest sustainable practices and policy measures to mitigate human-induced impacts on river ecosystems and protect aquatic biodiversity in India.
articles.imageDescription: A Gangetic Dolphin leaping out of river water.
articles.trivia: Gangetic Dolphins are functionally blind and rely on echolocation to navigate and hunt.

articles.headline: India-UK FTA: Remaining Sticking Points Addressed, Deal Nears Conclusion
articles.explainer:
• Negotiations for the India-United Kingdom Free Trade Agreement (FTA) have reportedly made significant progress, with both sides addressing most of the remaining contentious issues.
• Key areas of discussion include tariffs on certain goods, rules of origin, intellectual property rights, and market access for services.
• A successful FTA would open new opportunities for Indian exports, particularly in textiles, leather, and services, and attract UK investments.
• The deal aims to double bilateral trade by 2030, enhancing economic cooperation post-Brexit for the UK.
• Political will from both nations is driving the final push towards signing the ambitious trade pact.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India and the UK launched FTA negotiations in January 2022.
The UK is one of India's largest trading partners.
FTA aims to reduce trade barriers and boost economic ties.
Services sector is a major component of India-UK trade.
Post-Brexit, the UK is actively pursuing new trade deals globally.
mainsPoints: Analyze the economic and strategic rationale behind the India-UK Free Trade Agreement, considering the post-Brexit landscape for the UK and India's global trade ambitions.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges for key Indian sectors, such as textiles, automobiles, and services, arising from the FTA.
Examine how the FTA could impact India's foreign policy objectives and its role in shaping a multi-polar global economic order.
Evaluate the significance of 'rules of origin' and intellectual property rights in modern trade agreements and their implications for India.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the United Kingdom side-by-side.
articles.trivia: The UK was India's third-largest trading partner in 1992-93.

articles.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0: Progress in Waste Management and ODF+ Certification
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs reviewed the progress of Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 (SBM-U 2.0), focusing on sustainable solid waste management and sanitation.
• Significant strides have been made in achieving ODF+ and ODF++ certifications for urban local bodies, ensuring proper toilet usage and waste treatment.
• The mission emphasizes circular economy principles, promoting waste-to-wealth initiatives, source segregation, and material recovery facilities.
• Challenges include behavioral change, financial sustainability of waste management projects, and integration of informal waste pickers.
• The government aims to make all Indian cities 'Garbage Free' by 2026, aligning with broader sustainable development goals.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: SBM-U 2.0 was launched in October 2021 as a continuation of SBM-U.
Its objective is to make all cities 'Garbage Free' and 'Water Secure'.
ODF+ focuses on toilet usage with proper maintenance and sludge management.
ODF++ focuses on complete faecal sludge and septage management.
The mission promotes source segregation of waste into wet, dry, and hazardous categories.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the achievements and ongoing challenges of Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 in achieving sustainable solid waste management and urban sanitation.
Discuss the concept of 'circular economy' in the context of waste management and how SBM-U 2.0 is integrating its principles.
Examine the socio-economic impact of improved sanitation and waste management on urban populations, especially vulnerable sections.
Suggest policy interventions and technological solutions to overcome the hurdles in making Indian cities 'Garbage Free' and ensuring long-term sustainability of sanitation infrastructure.
articles.imageDescription: Urban waste management workers segregating waste.
articles.trivia: The first phase of Swachh Bharat Mission (Urban) achieved its target of making urban India Open Defecation Free (ODF) in 2019.

articles.headline: India Reviews Progress Towards 500 GW Renewable Energy Target by 2030
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy conducted a high-level review of India's progress towards achieving its ambitious target of 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
• The review highlighted significant additions in solar and wind power capacity, driven by supportive policies and private sector investment.
• Challenges include land acquisition, grid integration, storage solutions, and securing financing for large-scale projects.
• The government reiterated its commitment to accelerating renewable energy deployment to meet climate change goals and enhance energy security.
• Discussions also covered green hydrogen initiatives and their potential to decarbonize hard-to-abate sectors.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: India's target is 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
This target was announced at COP26 in Glasgow.
India is the world's third-largest energy consumer.
The International Solar Alliance (ISA) was co-founded by India.
National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's progress and challenges in achieving its 500 GW renewable energy target by 2030, considering technological, financial, and infrastructural aspects.
Discuss the role of renewable energy in enhancing India's energy security and meeting its commitments under the Paris Agreement on Climate Change.
Examine the potential of green hydrogen as a future energy source for India and the policy support required to scale up its production and adoption.
Evaluate the socio-economic implications of transitioning to a renewable energy-dominated grid, including job creation and impact on traditional energy sectors.
articles.imageDescription: Solar panels stretching into the horizon, symbolizing renewable energy.
articles.trivia: The world's largest solar park, Bhadla Solar Park, is located in Rajasthan, India.

articles.headline: Government Announces Third Phase of Smart Cities Mission, Focus on Digital Governance and Resilience
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced the launch of the third phase of the Smart Cities Mission, building upon the successes and lessons from previous phases.
• This new phase will focus on deeper integration of digital governance, climate resilience, and citizen-centric urban planning.
• Emphasis will be placed on leveraging data analytics for informed decision-making and developing smart infrastructure that can withstand environmental shocks.
• A key aspect will be promoting sustainable mobility solutions, green buildings, and robust public-private partnerships.
• The mission aims to create more livable, sustainable, and economically vibrant urban centers across the country.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Development processes and the development industry — the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission was launched in 2015.
It aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure and give a decent quality of life to its citizens.
100 cities were initially selected under the mission.
Integrated Command and Control Centres (ICCCs) are a key feature of Smart Cities.
The mission is under the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the achievements and shortcomings of the first two phases of the Smart Cities Mission, and how the third phase intends to address these.
Discuss the importance of digital governance and data-driven urban planning in creating resilient and citizen-centric smart cities in India.
Examine the challenges in financing and implementing large-scale urban infrastructure projects under the Smart Cities Mission, and suggest innovative funding models.
Analyze how climate resilience can be integrated into urban development strategies to safeguard Indian cities against the impacts of climate change.
articles.imageDescription: Modern cityscape with digital overlays, symbolizing a smart city.
articles.trivia: The concept of 'Smart City' involves using technology to improve urban services and quality of life.

articles.headline: India Boosts Indigenous Fighter Jet Production: Tejas MK-1A Deliveries Underway
articles.explainer:
• Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL) announced that deliveries of the Tejas MK-1A Light Combat Aircraft to the Indian Air Force are underway, marking a significant milestone in indigenous defense production.
• The Tejas MK-1A variant features advanced avionics, an AESA radar, and improved electronic warfare capabilities, enhancing its operational effectiveness.
• This move underscores India's commitment to 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' (self-reliant India) in defense manufacturing and reducing reliance on foreign imports.
• The increased production rate will help replace aging fleets and bolster the IAF's combat readiness.
• Further development of the Tejas MK-2 and Advanced Medium Combat Aircraft (AMCA) programs continues to push the boundaries of India's aerospace capabilities.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: Tejas is an indigenous Light Combat Aircraft (LCA) developed by Aeronautical Development Agency (ADA) and HAL.
The Tejas MK-1A is an advanced variant with improved capabilities.
HAL is a state-owned aerospace and defense company.
India aims to achieve self-reliance in defense manufacturing.
AESA radar is a type of phased array radar.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Tejas MK-1A deliveries for India's defense preparedness and its 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative in the defense sector.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in developing indigenous defense manufacturing capabilities, particularly in complex aerospace technologies.
Examine how the modernization of the Indian Air Force through indigenous platforms contributes to regional security dynamics and India's strategic autonomy.
Evaluate the role of public sector undertakings like HAL and DRDO in fostering research, development, and production in India's defense ecosystem.
articles.imageDescription: A Tejas Light Combat Aircraft in flight.
articles.trivia: Tejas is named after the Sanskrit word for 'Radiance'.

articles.headline: Global South Leaders Push for Enhanced Climate Finance at Pre-COP Summit
articles.explainer:
• Leaders from the Global South convened at a pre-COP summit, collectively calling for developed nations to fulfill their climate finance commitments and increase support for adaptation and mitigation efforts.
• The discussions highlighted the disproportionate impact of climate change on developing countries, despite their lower historical emissions.
• Key demands included operationalizing the Loss and Damage Fund, setting a new collective quantified goal for climate finance, and ensuring easier access to funds.
• India reiterated its stance on 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) in climate action.
• The summit aimed to build a unified front ahead of the upcoming UN Climate Change Conference (COP), emphasizing climate justice.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Bio-diversity and Climate Change.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
keyTerms: Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC)
prelimsPoints: COP (Conference of the Parties) is the supreme decision-making body of the UNFCCC.
The Loss and Damage Fund was established at COP27.
CBDR-RC is a principle under the UNFCCC.
Developed countries pledged to mobilize $100 billion per year by 2020 for climate action in developing countries.
India is a key voice for the Global South in climate negotiations.
mainsPoints: Analyze the demands of the Global South regarding climate finance and the principle of 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) in international climate negotiations.
Discuss the significance of the Loss and Damage Fund for developing countries and the challenges in its operationalization and funding.
Examine India's role and leadership in advocating for climate justice and equitable burden-sharing in global climate action.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current international mechanisms in mobilizing adequate climate finance for adaptation and mitigation in developing countries.
articles.imageDescription: Leaders from diverse nations at a conference table, symbolizing global climate talks.
articles.trivia: The term 'Global South' typically refers to countries in Africa, Latin America, and developing Asia.

articles.headline: National Health Authority Reports Increased Adoption of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs)
articles.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) announced a significant surge in the creation of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs), indicating growing public acceptance of digital health services.
• ABHA IDs are crucial for linking individual health records across different healthcare providers, facilitating seamless access to medical history and improving treatment coordination.
• The initiative is a cornerstone of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), aiming to create a comprehensive digital health ecosystem in India.
• Challenges include ensuring data privacy, interoperability across diverse systems, and reaching remote populations with limited digital literacy.
• The NHA is focusing on strengthening the digital infrastructure and expanding awareness campaigns to further accelerate adoption.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
articles.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identification number.
ABDM aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
The National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
It enables individuals to access and share their digital health records.
The mission covers health facilities, healthcare professionals, and citizens.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs) in transforming India's healthcare delivery system and improving patient outcomes.
Discuss the challenges associated with creating and maintaining a robust digital health ecosystem in a diverse country like India, including data privacy and interoperability.
Examine how the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission can contribute to universal health coverage and reduce health disparities across different socio-economic groups.
Suggest measures to enhance digital literacy and address the digital divide to ensure equitable access to digital health services for all citizens.
articles.imageDescription: A smartphone screen displaying a digital health ID with medical icons.
articles.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission was launched nationwide in September 2021.

articles.headline: India's Tourism Sector Shows Robust Recovery, Focus on Sustainable and Experiential Travel
articles.explainer:
• India's tourism sector reports a strong rebound, with both domestic and international tourist arrivals witnessing significant growth, surpassing pre-pandemic levels in several segments.
• The Ministry of Tourism is promoting sustainable and experiential tourism, focusing on rural tourism, eco-tourism, and spiritual circuits.
• Initiatives like 'Dekho Apna Desh' and 'Swadesh Darshan' are encouraging domestic travel and showcasing India's diverse cultural and natural heritage.
• Infrastructure development, including improved connectivity and hospitality services, plays a crucial role in supporting the growth.
• The sector is poised for further expansion with an emphasis on digital promotion and niche tourism segments.

articles.headline: Indian Antarctic Act, 2022: Implementation Strengthened for Environmental Protection and Research
articles.explainer:
• The Indian government is strengthening the implementation mechanisms of the Indian Antarctic Act, 2022, to regulate activities and protect the pristine Antarctic environment.
• The Act extends Indian laws to research stations and persons undertaking expeditions to Antarctica, ensuring compliance with international treaties like the Antarctic Treaty System.
• It mandates environmental impact assessments for any proposed activity, prohibits unauthorized drilling, and regulates waste disposal.
• India operates two permanent research stations in Antarctica: Maitri and Bharati, conducting scientific studies in various disciplines.
• The robust legal framework underscores India's commitment to responsible scientific research and environmental stewardship in polar regions.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: The Indian Antarctic Act, 2022 was passed to regulate visits and activities to Antarctica.
India is a signatory to the Antarctic Treaty, 1959.
India has two operational research stations in Antarctica: Maitri and Bharati.
Dakshin Gangotri was India's first permanent research station, now decommissioned.
The Act ensures compliance with the Protocol on Environmental Protection to the Antarctic Treaty (Madrid Protocol).
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Indian Antarctic Act, 2022, and its significance in regulating India's activities in Antarctica in line with international obligations.
Discuss the strategic and scientific importance of India's presence in Antarctica, highlighting the research conducted at Maitri and Bharati stations.
Examine the environmental challenges facing the Antarctic continent and how the Indian Antarctic Act contributes to its preservation.
Evaluate India's role in global polar research and its commitment to responsible governance of the continent under the Antarctic Treaty System.
articles.imageDescription: Indian research station in the snowy landscape of Antarctica.
articles.trivia: India launched its first expedition to Antarctica in 1981.

articles.headline: India Reviews Aspects of Indus Waters Treaty Amidst Hydropower Projects Development
articles.explainer:
• India is reportedly reviewing certain aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan, particularly concerning the development of hydropower projects on western rivers.
• The review aims to maximize India's utilization of its share of water resources allowed under the treaty for irrigation and power generation.
• Pakistan has raised objections to some of India's hydroelectric projects, citing concerns over water flow and treaty compliance.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank in 1960, is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties globally.
• India emphasizes its commitment to the treaty while asserting its rights to equitable and reasonable utilization of transboundary waters.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.

It was brokered by the World Bank.
The treaty allocated Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
India has limited rights to use Western Rivers for non-consumptive purposes like hydropower generation.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral commission for implementation of the treaty.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and key provisions of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) and its significance in India-Pakistan relations.
Discuss India's rights and limitations under the IWT regarding the utilization of Western River waters for hydropower projects.
Examine the challenges and potential for conflict resolution concerning transboundary water sharing, using the IWT as a case study.
Evaluate the role of international mediation, such as by the World Bank, in resolving water disputes between riparian states.

articles.headline: India Hosts G20 Sherpa Meeting, Focus on Sustainable Development and Digital Public Infrastructure
articles.explainer:
• India hosted a G20 Sherpa meeting, continuing its active engagement in the G20 troika, to prepare for future G20 summits and ministerial meetings.
• Discussions centered on advancing sustainable development goals, fostering economic resilience, and leveraging Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) for inclusive growth.
• India emphasized the need for global cooperation on climate action, food and energy security, and reforms of multilateral development banks.
• The meeting aimed to build consensus on key agenda items, reflecting the priorities of the Global South.
• India's role in the G20 continues to highlight its growing influence in shaping global economic and developmental discourse.

The G20 Sherpa is a personal representative of the head of state or government of a G20 member country.

Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) refers to digital systems and platforms that enable public and private services.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.

articles.headline: New Scheme to Revitalize Rural Economy Through Skill Development and Micro-Enterprises
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Rural Development launched a new flagship scheme aimed at revitalizing the rural economy through targeted skill development and promotion of micro-enterprises.
• The scheme focuses on empowering rural youth and women by providing training in market-relevant skills, access to credit, and mentorship for entrepreneurship.
• It integrates with existing programs like DAY-NRLM and PMKVY to create a synergistic approach for sustainable livelihoods.
• Addressing issues of rural unemployment, distress migration, and fostering local value chains are key objectives.
• The initiative seeks to build a resilient and self-sufficient rural India, contributing to inclusive national growth.

Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Micro-enterprises are small businesses with limited investment.

Financial inclusion is critical for rural entrepreneurship.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Human Rights Day, consider the following statements:
1. It is observed annually on 10th December to commemorate the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR).
2. India is a signatory to the UDHR and its Constitution enshrines Fundamental Rights in Part III.
3. The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a constitutional body established under Article 338 of the Indian Constitution.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: Human Rights Day is indeed observed on December 10 to mark the day the United Nations General Assembly adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights in 1948.
Statement 2 is correct: India is a signatory to the UDHR, and the Fundamental Rights are enshrined in Part III of the Indian Constitution.
Statement 3 is incorrect: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a statutory body, not a constitutional one. It was established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. Article 338 deals with the National Commission for Scheduled Castes.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for conducting the Monetary Policy Review in India?
options: (b) Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
(c) Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC)
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC)
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate (repo rate) to achieve the inflation target while keeping in mind the objective of growth. The Ministry of Finance handles fiscal policy, SEBI regulates capital markets, and NITI Aayog is a policy think tank.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Digital Personal Data Protection Bill, 2023:
1. It aims to establish a comprehensive legal framework for the processing of digital personal data in India.
2. It proposes the establishment of a Data Protection Board of India.
3. It applies only to government entities and excludes private sector organizations.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: The Digital Personal Data Protection Bill, 2023, is designed to provide a comprehensive legal framework for the processing of digital personal data.
Statement 2 is correct: The Bill indeed proposes the establishment of a Data Protection Board of India to enforce its provisions.
Statement 3 is incorrect: The Bill applies to both government and private sector entities (data fiduciaries) that process personal data.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to 'Project Dolphin', which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. It aims to conserve the Gangetic Dolphin, India's National Aquatic Animal.
2. The Gangetic Dolphin is listed as 'Critically Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
3. The project primarily focuses on marine dolphin species along India's coastline.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: Project Dolphin was launched to conserve the Gangetic Dolphin, which is India's National Aquatic Animal.
Statement 2 is incorrect: The Gangetic Dolphin is listed as 'Endangered' (not 'Critically Endangered') on the IUCN Red List.
Statement 3 is incorrect: Project Dolphin specifically focuses on the freshwater Gangetic Dolphin found in riverine ecosystems, not marine dolphin species.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) is/are correct?
1. The IWT was signed between India and Pakistan in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
2. It allocated the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India.
3. India has exclusive control over the waters of the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: The IWT was signed in 1960 with the World Bank as a mediator.
Statement 2 is correct: The treaty allocated the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) for India's unrestricted use.
Statement 3 is incorrect: The Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) were allocated to Pakistan, though India has limited rights to use their waters for non-consumptive purposes like hydropower generation and irrigation.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze India's commitment to human rights in the context of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR). Discuss the major challenges and recent initiatives taken to safeguard fundamental rights in the country. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure; Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population; Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.

mainsMocks.question: The launch of the 'India AI Mission' signifies a strategic move towards technological leadership. Discuss the potential of Artificial Intelligence to transform India's economy and society, while also addressing the ethical concerns and challenges in its responsible deployment. (250 words)

mainsMocks.approach: Start with the 'India AI Mission' objectives. Elaborate on AI's potential in sectors like healthcare, agriculture, governance, and economy (e.g., job creation, efficiency). Then, discuss ethical concerns (bias, privacy, surveillance, job displacement) and challenges (data infrastructure, skill gap, regulation). Conclude with the importance of a balanced, ethical, and inclusive AI strategy.

mainsMocks.question: Evaluate the effectiveness of the Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) monetary policy in achieving its twin objectives of price stability and economic growth in the current global and domestic economic scenario. What are the major challenges faced by the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC)? (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Government Budgeting; Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce RBI's twin objectives and the role of MPC. Discuss the current scenario (global uncertainties, domestic inflation). Analyze how repo rate decisions impact inflation and growth. Highlight challenges like supply-side inflation, global spillovers, policy transmission lags, and conflicting objectives. Conclude with the need for a nimble and forward-looking policy approach.

GS2.headline: Human Rights Day 2024: India Reiterates Commitment Amidst Persistent Challenges
GS2.explainer:
• December 10 marks Human Rights Day, commemorating the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) in 1948.
• India, a signatory to the UDHR, reaffirms its constitutional commitment to fundamental rights, enshrined in Part III of its Constitution.
• Discussions focus on progress in areas like right to education, food security, and judicial reforms, alongside challenges in protecting vulnerable groups, ensuring free speech, and addressing custodial violence.
• The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) emphasizes its role in monitoring and promoting human rights across the nation.
• International calls for greater accountability and adherence to global human rights standards are also highlighted.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: Human Rights Day is observed annually on December 10.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948.
India is a founding member of the UN and a signatory to the UDHR.
Part III of the Indian Constitution enshrines Fundamental Rights.
The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a statutory body established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze India's progress and challenges in upholding human rights since its independence, particularly in the context of the UDHR principles.
Discuss the role of the NHRC and other institutions in safeguarding human rights in India, identifying their strengths and limitations.
Examine the interplay between national sovereignty and international human rights obligations, using India as a case study.
Propose policy recommendations to enhance the protection of human rights for marginalized and vulnerable communities in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration of diverse hands holding a globe, symbolizing universal human rights.
GS2.trivia: The UDHR is the most translated document in the world, available in over 500 languages.

GS2.headline: Nobel Peace Prize 2024 Awarded in Oslo Ceremony
GS2.explainer:
• The Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 was formally presented in Oslo, Norway, on December 10, following the annual tradition.
• The recipient(s) were recognized for their outstanding contributions to peace, disarmament, or human rights.
• The ceremony highlighted the global challenges demanding peaceful resolutions and humanitarian efforts.
• The Nobel Committee's selection often aims to bring attention to pressing global issues and inspire further action.
• This year's award underscored the critical importance of international cooperation in an increasingly complex geopolitical landscape.

Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.

Alfred Nobel, the founder of the awards, specified the Peace Prize to be awarded by a Norwegian committee.

GS2.headline: Winter Session: Parliament Debates Key Bills on Digital Data Protection and Cooperative Sector Reforms
GS2.explainer:
• The Indian Parliament's Winter Session continues, with significant legislative activity focused on the Digital Personal Data Protection Bill, 2023, and amendments to the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act.
• The data protection bill aims to establish a comprehensive legal framework for the processing of digital personal data, ensuring privacy rights of individuals.
• Cooperative sector reforms seek to enhance governance, management, and financial health of cooperative societies across states.
• Opposition parties raise concerns over consultation processes and potential impacts on fundamental rights and federal structure.
• The government emphasizes the necessity of these reforms for economic growth and citizen welfare in the digital age.

It includes provisions for the establishment of a Data Protection Board of India.

Analyze the need for reforms in the cooperative sector and discuss how the proposed amendments to the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act address existing challenges.

GS2.headline: India-GCC Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Advance, Aiming for Early Conclusion
GS2.explainer:
• Negotiations for a Free Trade Agreement (FTA) between India and the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) are progressing positively, with both sides aiming for an early conclusion.
• The proposed FTA is expected to significantly boost bilateral trade and investment, especially in energy, infrastructure, and services sectors.
• The GCC, comprising six West Asian countries, is a crucial partner for India's energy security and a major destination for Indian expatriates.
• Discussions focus on market access for goods and services, investment protection, and dispute resolution mechanisms.
• An FTA is anticipated to diversify trade baskets and strengthen strategic ties between India and the GCC member states.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy 2020: Ministry Reviews Implementation Progress and Challenges
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education held a high-level meeting to review the implementation status of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 across states and Union Territories.
• Key areas of focus included the integration of vocational education, foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN), and the multi-disciplinary approach in higher education.
• Challenges such as infrastructure gaps, faculty training, and digital divide in remote areas were deliberated.
• The review highlighted successful initiatives in some states and stressed the need for collaborative efforts to achieve NEP's transformative goals.
• Emphasis was placed on leveraging technology for effective learning and assessment, aligning with the vision of 'Digital India'.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Academic Bank of Credits (ABC) is a key feature for higher education.
The policy aims for 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in preschool to secondary level by 2030.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Expresses Concern Over Delay in Judicial Appointments, Reiterates Collegium Recommendations
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India expressed strong reservations over the Union government's continued delay in clearing judicial appointments and transfers recommended by the Collegium.
• The Court reiterated the importance of adhering to the timelines set for the appointment process to ensure the efficient functioning of the judiciary.
• Vacancies in High Courts and the Supreme Court are impacting the pace of justice delivery, leading to increased pendency of cases.
• The issue highlights ongoing friction between the judiciary and the executive regarding the appointment mechanism.
• The Court emphasized that the independence of the judiciary is a cornerstone of the Constitution and must be preserved.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary; Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
prelimsPoints: The Collegium System is the system for appointment and transfer of judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.
It evolved through Supreme Court judgments (First, Second, and Third Judges Cases).
The Chief Justice of India (CJI) heads the Supreme Court Collegium.
Article 124 and Article 217 deal with the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court and High Courts, respectively.
The National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) Act was struck down by the Supreme Court in 2015.
mainsPoints: Critically examine the Collegium system for judicial appointments in India, highlighting its merits and demerits in ensuring judicial independence and accountability.
Discuss the implications of delayed judicial appointments on the efficiency of the justice delivery system and the concept of rule of law in India.
Analyze the ongoing debate between the executive and judiciary regarding judicial appointments, suggesting possible reforms to streamline the process.
Evaluate the principle of separation of powers in the context of judicial appointments and the need for a balanced approach between different organs of the government.

GS2.trivia: The Supreme Court of India officially came into being on January 28, 1950.

GS2.headline: India-UK FTA: Remaining Sticking Points Addressed, Deal Nears Conclusion

• Key areas of discussion include tariffs on certain goods, rules of origin, intellectual property rights, and market access for services.
• A successful FTA would open new opportunities for Indian exports, particularly in textiles, leather, and services, and attract UK investments.

• Political will from both nations is driving the final push towards signing the ambitious trade pact.

GS2.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0: Progress in Waste Management and ODF+ Certification
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs reviewed the progress of Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 (SBM-U 2.0), focusing on sustainable solid waste management and sanitation.
• Significant strides have been made in achieving ODF+ and ODF++ certifications for urban local bodies, ensuring proper toilet usage and waste treatment.
• The mission emphasizes circular economy principles, promoting waste-to-wealth initiatives, source segregation, and material recovery facilities.
• Challenges include behavioral change, financial sustainability of waste management projects, and integration of informal waste pickers.
• The government aims to make all Indian cities 'Garbage Free' by 2026, aligning with broader sustainable development goals.

GS2.headline: Government Announces Third Phase of Smart Cities Mission, Focus on Digital Governance and Resilience
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced the launch of the third phase of the Smart Cities Mission, building upon the successes and lessons from previous phases.
• This new phase will focus on deeper integration of digital governance, climate resilience, and citizen-centric urban planning.
• Emphasis will be placed on leveraging data analytics for informed decision-making and developing smart infrastructure that can withstand environmental shocks.
• A key aspect will be promoting sustainable mobility solutions, green buildings, and robust public-private partnerships.
• The mission aims to create more livable, sustainable, and economically vibrant urban centers across the country.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Development processes and the development industry — the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission was launched in 2015.
It aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure and give a decent quality of life to its citizens.
100 cities were initially selected under the mission.
Integrated Command and Control Centres (ICCCs) are a key feature of Smart Cities.
The mission is under the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the achievements and shortcomings of the first two phases of the Smart Cities Mission, and how the third phase intends to address these.
Discuss the importance of digital governance and data-driven urban planning in creating resilient and citizen-centric smart cities in India.
Examine the challenges in financing and implementing large-scale urban infrastructure projects under the Smart Cities Mission, and suggest innovative funding models.
Analyze how climate resilience can be integrated into urban development strategies to safeguard Indian cities against the impacts of climate change.
GS2.imageDescription: Modern cityscape with digital overlays, symbolizing a smart city.
GS2.trivia: The concept of 'Smart City' involves using technology to improve urban services and quality of life.

GS2.headline: National Health Authority Reports Increased Adoption of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs)
GS2.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) announced a significant surge in the creation of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs), indicating growing public acceptance of digital health services.
• ABHA IDs are crucial for linking individual health records across different healthcare providers, facilitating seamless access to medical history and improving treatment coordination.
• The initiative is a cornerstone of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), aiming to create a comprehensive digital health ecosystem in India.
• Challenges include ensuring data privacy, interoperability across diverse systems, and reaching remote populations with limited digital literacy.
• The NHA is focusing on strengthening the digital infrastructure and expanding awareness campaigns to further accelerate adoption.

Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

ABDM aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
The National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
It enables individuals to access and share their digital health records.
The mission covers health facilities, healthcare professionals, and citizens.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs) in transforming India's healthcare delivery system and improving patient outcomes.
Discuss the challenges associated with creating and maintaining a robust digital health ecosystem in a diverse country like India, including data privacy and interoperability.
Examine how the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission can contribute to universal health coverage and reduce health disparities across different socio-economic groups.
Suggest measures to enhance digital literacy and address the digital divide to ensure equitable access to digital health services for all citizens.

GS2.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission was launched nationwide in September 2021.

GS2.headline: India Reviews Aspects of Indus Waters Treaty Amidst Hydropower Projects Development
GS2.explainer:
• India is reportedly reviewing certain aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan, particularly concerning the development of hydropower projects on western rivers.
• The review aims to maximize India's utilization of its share of water resources allowed under the treaty for irrigation and power generation.
• Pakistan has raised objections to some of India's hydroelectric projects, citing concerns over water flow and treaty compliance.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank in 1960, is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties globally.
• India emphasizes its commitment to the treaty while asserting its rights to equitable and reasonable utilization of transboundary waters.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed between India and Pakistan in 1960.
It was brokered by the World Bank.
The treaty allocated Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
India has limited rights to use Western Rivers for non-consumptive purposes like hydropower generation.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral commission for implementation of the treaty.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and key provisions of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) and its significance in India-Pakistan relations.
Discuss India's rights and limitations under the IWT regarding the utilization of Western River waters for hydropower projects.
Examine the challenges and potential for conflict resolution concerning transboundary water sharing, using the IWT as a case study.
Evaluate the role of international mediation, such as by the World Bank, in resolving water disputes between riparian states.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized map showing the Indus River system and its tributaries.
GS2.trivia: The Indus River originates in the Tibetan Plateau, near Lake Manasarovar.

GS2.headline: India Hosts G20 Sherpa Meeting, Focus on Sustainable Development and Digital Public Infrastructure
GS2.explainer:
• India hosted a G20 Sherpa meeting, continuing its active engagement in the G20 troika, to prepare for future G20 summits and ministerial meetings.
• Discussions centered on advancing sustainable development goals, fostering economic resilience, and leveraging Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) for inclusive growth.
• India emphasized the need for global cooperation on climate action, food and energy security, and reforms of multilateral development banks.
• The meeting aimed to build consensus on key agenda items, reflecting the priorities of the Global South.
• India's role in the G20 continues to highlight its growing influence in shaping global economic and developmental discourse.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: G20 is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
The G20 Sherpa is a personal representative of the head of state or government of a G20 member country.
The G20 Troika consists of the current, immediate past, and immediate future G20 presidencies.
Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) refers to digital systems and platforms that enable public and private services.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's objectives and priorities as a key member of the G20, particularly concerning sustainable development and the promotion of Digital Public Infrastructure.
Discuss the significance of G20 Sherpa meetings in shaping the agenda and outcomes of the broader G20 summit process.
Examine India's advocacy for the Global South within the G20 framework and its efforts to reform multilateral development banks.
Evaluate the potential of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) to drive inclusive growth and achieve Sustainable Development Goals globally, with India's experience as a reference.
GS2.imageDescription: G20 logo on a conference backdrop with flags of member nations.
GS2.trivia: The G20 accounts for around 85% of global GDP, over 75% of global trade, and about two-thirds of the world population.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Global Uncertainties and Domestic Inflation Concerns
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its latest review, deciding to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged.
• The decision comes against a backdrop of persistent global economic uncertainties, including geopolitical tensions and fluctuating commodity prices.
• Domestic inflation, particularly in food items, remains a key concern, though core inflation shows signs of moderation.
• The RBI emphasized its commitment to achieving the medium-term inflation target while supporting economic growth.
• Experts anticipate a 'wait and watch' approach from the central bank, with future rate cuts dependent on sustained disinflation and global economic stability.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body headed by the RBI Governor.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims for CPI inflation of 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Quantitative easing and tightening are tools used by central banks.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate, considering both domestic and international economic factors.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with the objective of supporting economic growth in the current macroeconomic environment.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflationary pressures, particularly in food items.
Examine the impact of global economic uncertainties, such as geopolitical conflicts and commodity price volatility, on India's monetary policy decisions.
GS3.imageDescription: Image of the Reserve Bank of India headquarters building in Mumbai.
GS3.trivia: The RBI was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: India AI Mission Launched to Boost AI Research, Development, and Ethical Deployment
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian government officially launched the 'India AI Mission', a comprehensive initiative aimed at accelerating research, development, and ethical deployment of Artificial Intelligence.
• The mission includes establishing Centers of Excellence for AI, fostering AI startups, developing high-end computing infrastructure, and creating a robust data ecosystem.
• Emphasis is placed on responsible AI development, addressing ethical concerns, data privacy, and potential societal impacts.
• The initiative seeks to position India as a global leader in AI innovation, contributing to various sectors like healthcare, agriculture, and governance.
• Public-private partnerships and international collaborations are envisioned as key pillars for the mission's success.

Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.

Examine the potential impact of AI on employment patterns, skill requirements, and the future of work in India, and how the mission can mitigate negative consequences.
GS3.imageDescription: Stylized graphic representing Artificial Intelligence with circuits and a brain icon.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

GS3.headline: Project Dolphin: Conservation Efforts Intensified for National Aquatic Animal
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change announced intensified efforts under 'Project Dolphin' to protect the Gangetic Dolphin, India's National Aquatic Animal.
• Measures include habitat protection, curbing poaching, reducing by-catch, and raising awareness among local communities along riverine ecosystems.
• The Gangetic Dolphin, an indicator species of river health, faces threats from pollution, dam construction, and unsustainable fishing practices.
• Advanced technologies, including satellite tagging and eDNA monitoring, are being employed for better population assessment and tracking.
• Collaboration with riparian states and international bodies is crucial for the long-term survival of the species.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Bio-diversity and Climate Change.
prelimsPoints: The Gangetic Dolphin (Platanista gangetica) is India's National Aquatic Animal.
It is listed as 'Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
Project Dolphin was launched by the Prime Minister in 2020.
It is a freshwater dolphin found in the Ganga-Brahmaputra-Meghna river system.
Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Sanctuary in Bihar is India's only dolphin sanctuary.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of the Gangetic Dolphin as an indicator species and the various threats it faces in its natural habitat.
Evaluate the effectiveness of 'Project Dolphin' in conserving the species, outlining its key strategies and the challenges in their implementation.
Examine the role of community participation and inter-state cooperation in successful wildlife conservation efforts, with specific reference to riverine species.
Suggest sustainable practices and policy measures to mitigate human-induced impacts on river ecosystems and protect aquatic biodiversity in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A Gangetic Dolphin leaping out of river water.
GS3.trivia: Gangetic Dolphins are functionally blind and rely on echolocation to navigate and hunt.

GS3.headline: India Reviews Progress Towards 500 GW Renewable Energy Target by 2030
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy conducted a high-level review of India's progress towards achieving its ambitious target of 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
• The review highlighted significant additions in solar and wind power capacity, driven by supportive policies and private sector investment.
• Challenges include land acquisition, grid integration, storage solutions, and securing financing for large-scale projects.
• The government reiterated its commitment to accelerating renewable energy deployment to meet climate change goals and enhance energy security.
• Discussions also covered green hydrogen initiatives and their potential to decarbonize hard-to-abate sectors.

Evaluate the socio-economic implications of transitioning to a renewable energy-dominated grid, including job creation and impact on traditional energy sectors.
